assessment.summary: A rare but recurring natural hazard with a documented modern precedent (1989 Quebec blackout) and a credible worst case — a Carrington-level storm today could leave tens of millions without power for weeks to years and cost an estimated ~$2 trillion, per NOAA — against power grids that are not yet built to withstand it.
assessment.timeframe: Carrington-level storms are characterized by USGS as "100-year-type events" — rare but not remote on an infrastructure-planning timescale, and effectively inevitable over a long enough horizon.

claims.text: These large [Carrington-level] storms are 100-year-type events, so although rare, they are still potentially consequential; in some areas, expected magnetic field strengths would be enough to stress or damage high-voltage transformers and potentially lead to massive power outages, with the Eastern and Midwestern U.S. most affected.

A solar storm of Carrington magnitude today has the potential to cost nearly $2 trillion, disrupting telecommunications, banking systems, GPS, and the energy grid; the estimated total U.S. population at risk of an extended power outage from a Carrington-level storm is between 20-40 million, with durations of 16 days to 1-2 years.
During the March 1989 geomagnetic storm, auroral currents shorted out the Quebec power grid and within 90 seconds, without warning, six million people were left without power for nine hours.

description: A severe solar-driven disturbance of Earth's magnetic field, triggered by a coronal mass ejection, capable of inducing damaging currents in long conductors such as power-grid transmission lines, pipelines, and undersea cables, and disrupting satellites, GPS, and radio communications. A Carrington-class (1859) event has not repeated in the modern electrified era, but near-misses (2012) and smaller storms (1989 Quebec blackout) show the underlying hazard is real and recurring.
name: Extreme Geomagnetic Storm
